planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; a traditional Mexican dried chile harvested red and used primarily dried — do not harvest green. Zone 8–11 gardeners can grow this easily as a spring crop in the ground.

directions: Start seeds at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant to the garden in warm soil after all frost risk has passed. Cascabel — named for the rattle the seeds make inside the dried pod — is a round, 1.5-inch, deep red-brown dried Mexican chile with mild to moderate heat (1,500–2,500 Scoville), a uniquely nutty, slightly smoky, woody flavor with an earthy depth that is used in traditional Mexican moles, salsas, and braised meats; it is harvested fully red on the plant and dried for 2–3 weeks in a warm, airy location. Plants are compact and bushy, producing round pods in clusters; the fully dried pods have a distinctive rattling sound from the loose seeds inside, making them easy to identify in a pantry. Grow in full sun with consistent moisture to maximize fruit set.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; a widely used dried Mexican chile that requires a long warm season — ideal for zone 8–11 where it can produce two harvests in warm years.

directions: Start seeds at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost, harden transplants thoroughly, and plant into warm, well-drained, moderately fertile soil after all frost risk has passed. Guajillo is one of the most commonly used dried chiles in traditional Mexican cooking, the dried form of the Mirasol pepper, producing long (3–5 inch), tapered, shiny red fruits with smooth, thin skin that dries to a deep red-brown; heat is mild to medium (2,500–5,000 SHU). The distinctive sweet, tangy, slightly fruity flavor with mild heat makes guajillo an indispensable base for authentic red enchilada sauce, pozole rojo, and adobo marinades. Harvest fully red and dry slowly on the plant or by hanging in a warm, ventilated space; dried guajillos store for 6–12 months.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; the chilaca/pasilla is used fresh when dark green and dried (as pasilla) for authentic Mexican sauces and moles — most gardeners grow it specifically for drying.

directions: Start seeds at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant into warm, fertile, well-drained soil after all frost danger has passed. Pasilla Bajio — meaning 'little raisin from the lowlands' — is the dried form of the fresh chilaca chile, producing narrow, 6–9 inch, wrinkled dark green to near-black fruits that dry to a very dark reddish-black with a distinctive fruity, dried-fruit, earthy flavor and medium heat (1,000–2,500 SHU). Pasilla is one of the three essential dried chiles used together in authentic Mexican mole negro (along with mulato and ancho) and is used extensively in Oaxacan and central Mexican cooking. The tall, upright plants produce cascading clusters of long, drooping fruits; harvest green for fresh use or allow to ripen fully dark and dry on the plant.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after all frost risk has passed; multiple varieties exist under the 'Hatch' name (Big Jim, Joe Parker, 6-4, Sandia) — all are New Mexico-bred long green chiles best harvested green and roasted.

directions: Start seeds at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant into warm, well-drained soil rich in phosphorus after all frost risk has passed. Hatch green chile refers to New Mexico-style long green chiles — varieties such as NuMex Big Jim, Joe Parker, and the heritage 6-4 — bred at New Mexico State University and grown in the Hatch Valley of southern New Mexico; they produce 6–10 inch, elongated, thick-walled green pods with heat levels ranging from mild to hot depending on cultivar and growing conditions. The distinctive flavor comes from the dry, high-altitude climate, but home gardeners in zones 7–11 with hot, dry summers can produce excellent results in-ground. Harvest green when pods are fully sized and char-blister them on a grill or under a broiler; the roasted flavor is unmatched for green chile sauces, stews, and salsas.
sources: Chile Pepper Institute, New Mexico State University
New Mexico State University Cooperative Extension

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; an African-American heirloom pepper from Philadelphia with striking color changes through the ripening cycle — highly ornamental as well as edible.

directions: Start seeds at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost and transplant into warm, fertile, well-drained soil after all frost risk has passed. Buena Mulata is a treasured African-American heirloom pepper with roots in Philadelphia's Black community, saved for generations and brought to wider attention through Seed Savers Exchange; it produces thin, cayenne-shaped pods 4–5 inches long that pass through a remarkable spectrum of colors as they ripen — pale cream to lavender-purple to orange to red — making the plant highly ornamental at every stage. The flavor is moderately hot (about 30,000 SHU) with a distinctive spicy, slightly fruity complexity; use fresh at any stage or dry to a rich red powder. This is a historically significant cultivar that deserves preservation and wider growing.
